coredata.dc:title: Efficient resistance against solid-state quenching of carbon dots towards white light emitting diodes by physical embedding into silica
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junli
Zhang, Feng
Wang, Yaling
Yang, Yongzhen
Liu, Xuguang
coredata.dc:description: To overcome self-quenching of carbon dots (CDs) in solid state and provide a universal strategy for efficient luminescence of CDs in solid state, this paper reports a novel method of physical embedding of solid-state CDs into a silica matrix. CD/silica composites were prepared through injecting N-(3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl) ethylenediamine into a CD aqueous solution and have exhibited a high quantum yield of 41.72%. A mechanism of photoluminscence (PL) quenching in solid-state CDs and PL resuming in CD/silica composites was proposed. These CD/silica composites possess excellent film-forming ability, thermostability and ultraviolet (UV) stability. By taking advantage of these remarkable properties, a white light-emitting diode was constructed by combining CD/silica film with a UV chip, which has exhibited warm white light emission with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage coordinates of (0.44, 0.42) and correlated color temperature of 2951 K. It is evident that CD/silica composites have superior potential in solid-state lighting system.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The morphology and structure of CDs and CD/silica composites
Optical properties of CDs and CD/silica composites
Mechanism of the PL quenching and resuming

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology
Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U1610255 , U1607120 ), the Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology ( 2015013002-10 , 201605D131045-10 ), and the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program ( 201603D111010 and 201703D321015-1 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of synthesizing CDs and CD/silica film along with the application in white LEDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM image, the histogram and Gaussian fittings of particle size distribution and HRTEM image of CDs. (b) TEM and HRTEM images of CD/silica composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) FT-IR spectra of PPDA and CDs. (b) FT-IR spectra of KH-792 and CD/silica composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra of the CD ethanol solution. (b) PL emission spectra of CD ethanol solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The photographs of CD ethanol solution (a, a’) and CD powder (b, b’) under daylight lamp (up) and UV lamp (down). The pictures of CD/silica film (c, c’) and CD/silica powder (d, d’) under daylight lamp (up) and UV lamp (down). (e) The PL spectra of CD solution and CD powder. PL spectra of CD/silica film (f) and CD/silica powder (g) under excitation with different wavelengths (see the inset legends). (h) UV–vis spectra of the hydrolysis product of KH-792. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) PL spectral emission of CD ethanol solution with different concentrations. (b) Concentration dependent behavior of CDs in ethanol solution. TEM images of concentration-induced aggregation of the as-prepared CDs. The concentration of CD ethanol solution is 0.001 mg/mL (c), 0.01 mg/mL (d), 0.1 mg/mL (e). (f) Spectral overlap between absorption spectrum and emission spectrum of CD ethanol solution. (g) The distribution of CDs in CD/silica composites. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The fluorescence-decay and fitting curves of CD ethanol solution (0.001 and 1 mg/mL) and CD powder. (b) The fluorescence-decay and fitting curves of CD/silica film and CD/silica powder (the laser excitation wavelength used is 375 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic of the self-quenching in solid-state CDs and the resistance against solid-state self-quenching of CDs by embedding into silica matrix. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TG curves of CDs and CD/silica composites. (b) PL spectra of CD/silica film under different temperatures. (c) Variation of the measured PL intensity of CD/silica composites versus temperature. The temperature is increased from 30 to 150 °C in steps of 20 °C. (d) Evolution of PL intensity of CD/silica composites under UV light for 60 min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic view of the LED based on CD/silica composites. (b) Emission spectra of white LED under different working voltages. (c) The emission spectrum of white LED. Inset is the photograph of the working white LED with warm white emission. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram and CCT for the white LED. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Fluorescence-decay lifetimes and fitting parameters of CD ethanol solution (0.001 and 1 mg/mL), CD powder, CD/silica film and CD/silica powder.

head.ce:title: Efficient resistance against solid-state quenching of carbon dots towards white light emitting diodes by physical embedding into silica

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center on Advanced Materials Science and Technology, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials, (Taiyuan University of Technology), Ministry of Education, Taiyuan 030024, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interface Science and Engineering in Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To overcome self-quenching of carbon dots (CDs) in solid state and provide a universal strategy for efficient luminescence of CDs in solid state, this paper reports a novel method of physical embedding of solid-state CDs into a silica matrix. CD/silica composites were prepared through injecting N-(3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl) ethylenediamine into a CD aqueous solution and have exhibited a high quantum yield of 41.72%. A mechanism of photoluminscence (PL) quenching in solid-state CDs and PL resuming in CD/silica composites was proposed. These CD/silica composites possess excellent film-forming ability, thermostability and ultraviolet (UV) stability. By taking advantage of these remarkable properties, a white light-emitting diode was constructed by combining CD/silica film with a UV chip, which has exhibited warm white light emission with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage coordinates of (0.44, 0.42) and correlated color temperature of 2951 K. It is evident that CD/silica composites have superior potential in solid-state lighting system.

The solid-state CDs were physically embedded into silica matrix to realize efficient luminescence of CD powder.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: White light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are attracting a great deal of attention and their commercialization is expanding to high-volume applications, including general illumination and displays, owing to the merits including efficient lighting, environment-friendly nature and long operational life [1]. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), as emerging quantum dots, are intriguingly increasing attention for their outstanding properties, such as excellent fluorescence, photostability, and low toxicity, which make them a promising color converter for application in white LEDs [2,3]. However, strong luminescence quenching in solid-state CDs and lack of long-wavelength emission (in orange and red light regions) in CDs greatly hinder the development and application of CDs-based white LEDs.

To support white LEDs, CDs are needed to emit light in solid state, but most of CD-related studies have been focused on fluorescence of CD solution. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore the solid-state fluorescence of CDs [4,5]. At present, CD-based LEDs are still not satisfactory in their performance owing to strong luminescence quenching in their solid aggregate state [6,7]. Usually, dispersant is needed to realize luminescence in solid-state CDs. Recently, an approach of embedding CDs in solid matrices, such as starch, salts and barium sulfate has been adopted in order to avoid solid-state fluorescence quenching and realize solid-state luminescence of CD-based composites [8–10]. Although the strong photoluminscence (PL) emission of CDs is well preserved in the solid state, curing agent must be further added into CD-based composites to fabricate LEDs [8]. Other several methods have also been proposed, where CDs were embedded in polymer matrices, such as polyvinyl alcohol and polyvinyl pyrrolidone [11,12]. However, polyvinyl alcohol and polyvinyl pyrrolidone are solid powder and need to be heated to form colloids to disperse CD powder. In addition, silica coating has been reported to realize the solid PL of perovskite quantum dots (QDs), in which perovskite QD/silica composites were formed in one-pot synthesis by covalently bonding silica and perovskite QDs [13]. Furthermore, to the best of our knowledge, although solid PL of CDs has been realized by combining with curing agent in some reports, there is rare, if any, report on the PL of same CDs in different systems (CD solution, CD powder and CD/curing agent composites). Thus, it is a challenge to explore a novel physical embedding method to realize the solid-state PL on the basis of the elucidation of the mechanism of solid-state PL of CDs. Meanwhile, the film-forming ability of CDs is also expected without additional curing agent.

On the other hand, in most cases, CDs exhibit broadband luminescence with maxima in blue–green region. For warm white LEDs requiring correlated color temperature (CCT) of 2700–4000 K [14], effective synthesis methods and convincing luminescence mechanisms are essential for CDs to realize long wavelength emission [15,16]. Although Qu et al. pointed that large particle sizes and high nitrogen contents are responsible for the efficient red emission [17], the specific mechanism about long-wavelength emission of CDs is still obscure. Therefore, it is also important for CDs to realize efficient orange or red luminescence for the fabrication of warm white LEDs and further explore the mechanism about long-wavelength emission of CDs.

This paper presents a new and effective technology for solid-state PL and film-forming CDs in order to prevent the self-quenching of CDs in solid state. N-(3-(Trimethoxysilyl) propyl) ethylenediamine (KH-792) was chosen as the precursor simultaneously for silica matrix as disperse agent and for silanol as film-forming agent. CD/silica composites were formed by direct injection of KH-792 into a CD aqueous solution. In this way, the silica matrix was formed fastly and CDs were embedded into silica by physical doping between CDs and silica. This novel approach has three advantages in realizing solid-state luminescence of CDs applied in LEDs. (1) KH-792 hydrolyzes and polymerizes into the silica, and CDs are further embedded into silica matrix to form CD/silica composites. Thus, these CD particles in CD/silica composites keep an appropriate distance away from each other so that the solid quenching can be restrained. (2) The as-prepared CD/silica composites can be directly tuned into a fluorescent film only through a simple heat-treatment without using any other curing agent. The fabrication process is simple and easy to scale up [18]. (3) The hydrolysis product of KH-792 has no observable absorption between 350 and 750 nm (proved below, Fig. 5h), so the matrix absorbs neither excitation light to CDs nor the emission from CDs, and optical properties of CDs can be unaffected after the solidification. CDs with orange-to-red fluorescence in an ethanol solution were synthesized by the reaction of p-phenylenediamine (PPDA) in ethanol medium using a one-step solvothermal process with reaction time being adjusted from 12 h to 6 h by optimization of reaction parameters [19]. Nevertheless, the CD powder directly derived from CD solution suffered fluorescence quenching from their aggregated states. Followed by dispersing the solid CDs in KH-792 aqueous solution, newly formed CD/silica composites showed resumed PL emission. The CD/silica film from heat-drying of CD/silica aqueous solution also reserved PL emission. Then, CD/silica film was combined with ultraviolet (UV) chip to fabricate a white LED. Fig. 1 illuminates schematic diagram of synthesizing CDs and CD/silica film along with the application in white LEDs.

PPDA was purchased from Xingfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). KH-792, ethanol absolute and methylene dichloride were offered from Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Methanol anhydrous was obtained from Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Petroleum ether was provided from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were used without further purification.

CDs were synthesized according to a solvothermal method reported previously [19]. Typically, PPDA (0.45 g) was dissolved in 45 mL of ethanol to form a transparent solution, which was sealed into a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves followed by a solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the color of the solution was turned into dark red. This solution including CDs and by-products was critically separated through silica column chromatography using a mixture of methylene chloride and methanol (volume ratio = 30/1) as eluent because CDs and by-products have different polarities. Finally, bright orange-red fluorescent CDs were obtained after removal of solvent under vacuum.

CDs (2 mg) were dissolved in 2 mL of water, and then 500 μL of KH-792 was added into the solution under sonication for 3 minutes to form a homogenous solution. The CD/silica film was obtained by a facile heating (80 °C) using quartz chip as substrate, and the CD/silica powder was obtained through a facile heating (80 °C) using a centrifuge tube as substrate and a grinding process for further characterization.

A white LED was fabricated by using the CD/silica film as luminescent materials and UV chip with the peak emission wavelength centered at 365 nm as LED base. Typically, 40 μL of CD/silica solution was transferred onto the inner wall of the optical lens, and the combined package was treated in a drying oven at 80 °C. Finally, the optical lens were capped on UV chip.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEOL JEM-2010 microscope. The TEM specimens were prepared by sonicating CD and CD/silica ethanol solutions and depositing a few drops of the solutions onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying in the oven. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using an NSK SPA-300 H V microscope. The AFM specimens of CD/silica composites were prepared by depositing a few drops of CD/silica solution on a quartz surface. Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) analysis was performed on a Bruker micrOTOF-Q III, in which the CDs were dissolved in ethanol with an infusion rate of 3 μL/min. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a Kratos AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with mono X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained from a BRUKER TENSOR 27 spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets. Thermo-gravimetric (TG) curves were measured on a NETZSCH TG 209 F3 with a heating rate of 10 °C per minute under nitrogen. Elemental analysis (EA) measurements were conducted on an ELEMENTAR vario EL cube. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer. PL spectra were collected on a Horiba Fluoromax-4 luminescence spectrometer using a Xe lamp as excitation source. The fluorescence decay status and lifetime were obtained on an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer. A spectra scan PR 655 photometer was applied to analyze the emission spectra, Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates and CCT of the white LED.

QY of the CD solution was measured according to a relative method. Rhodamine B aqueous solution was selected as the standard to determine relative QY of CD ethanol solution. All the absorbance values of the solutions were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 505 nm. In order to minimize internal filter effects, optical absorbance values of CD solution and reference fluorescence dye solution with different concentration were kept less than 0.05 [20]. The integrated emission intensity was calculated according to the area under the PL curve with the wavelength range of 525–750 nm. Then, through plotting the integrated emission intensity against the absorbance, it can be readily seen that the integrated emission intensity is a linear function of absorbance. The QY of CDs was calculated according to the following equation Eq (1):
(1)
Q =
Q
s t
×
K
K
s t
×
(
n
n
s t
)
2
where Q is the QY of CDs, K is the slope from the plot of the integrated emission intensity against the absorbance, and the subscript “st” refers to standard. n is the refractive index of the solvent.

The absolute QY of CD solution, CD powder, CD/silica film and CD/silica powder was tested using an integrating sphere, which was attached to a lifetime and steady state spectrometer. First, in order to minimize internal filter effects, CD ethanol solution was diluted to an absorption intensity below 0.08 at the optimal excitation wavelength of 505 nm [20]. Then, two 10 mm fluorescence cuvettes were separately used to fill equal volume of CD ethanol solution and pure ethanol as contrast. Subsequently, CD ethanol solution was placed in the integrating sphere and excited with monochromatic light at 505 nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the ranges of 495–515 and 525–750 nm. Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of pure ethanol were recorded under identical conditions. Finally, fluorescence software was used to calculate QY according to the spectra of sample and pure ethanol. The CD powder, CD/silica film and CD/silica powder were first placed in integrating sphere and excited at 505 nm. Then, the fluorescence spectra of blank base were also recorded under identical conditions. Lastly, the fluorescence spectra were recorded in the ranges of 495–515 and 525–750 nm to calculate the QY of CD powder, CD/silica film and CD/silica powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The morphology and structure of CDs and CD/silica composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized according to a previously reported solvothermal method, but with newly optimized reaction parameters [19]. The reaction temperature and time for the strongest fluorescence were determined to be 180 °C and 6 h (Fig. S1 and Tables S1 and S2). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the morphology and size of CDs, as characterized by TEM, illuminate that the CDs are spherical with the size in the range of 4.0–9.0 nm centered at 6.5–7.0 nm. The HRTEM image of CDs shows that the CD nanoparticles possess a crystal structure. From CDs, well-resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing 0.27 nm are observed, which are close to the (020) diffraction facets of graphite carbon [21]. The CD/silica composites were also analyzed by TEM, revealing that CDs are evenly embedded into silica matrix. The HRTEM image of a single CD shows that it has a lattice spacing of 0.27 nm (Fig. 2b), the same as pure CDs. In addition, it is suggested that the matrix next to CDs with amorphous structure may be silica in HRTEM image of CD/silica composites, which can be further confirmed by the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3 b) of CD/silica composites.

The EA was employed to characterize elemental types and contents of CDs. The mass fractions of carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and oxygen are measured to be 51.34%, 5.02%, 14.72% and 28.92% (calculated by the subtraction method), respectively. XPS measurements were performed to determine the surface elemental characteristics of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S2a, the survey scan of the CDs presents three apparent peaks centered at 284.80, 398.45, and 532.80 eV, corresponding to C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively. The expanded spectrum of C1s shown in Fig. S2b illustrates three peaks at 283.92, 284.64 and 285.28 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO and CN/CO, respectively [22]. The N1s spectrum seen in Fig. S2c exhibits three peaks centered at 398.76, 399.76 and 398.12 eV, attributed to N H, CN and CN, respectively. Consistent with C1s spectrum, O1s spectrum also confirms the existence of CO (532.21 eV) and COH/C O C (532.99 eV) as shown in Fig. S2d [2,23]. In addition, the relative atom contents of C, N and O are calculated as 59.94%, 18.37% and 21.68%, respectively (from XPS measurements). To further investigate the surface state of CDs, the solvent-dependent behavior of these CDs was tested. The polarity of the solvent decreases along the following order of water, dimethyl formamide (DMF), ethanol, acetone, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate and dichloromethane (DCM). As can be seen in Fig. S3, the emission wavelength and emission intensity depend on the polarity of solvent, and this solvent effect may result from the interactions between solvent and PL center of CDs [24]. Fig. S4 displays the photograph of CDs dispersed in different solvents, which indicates that CDs can be well dispersed in diverse organic solvents. It is plausible that PL emission (molecular state) originates from abundant functional groups. Those organic functional groups, such as CO, CN, and O and N-related defect states, are formed on the surface of CDs as the reaction progresses, and confer the CDs with excellent dispersibility and intense emission in diverse organic solvents [14].

Recent advances have shown that the CDs from hydrothermal synthesis of molecular precursors probably consist of small molecular species [25]. In order to investigate the molecular composition of CDs, CDs were measured by ESI-MS. As seen from Fig. S5a, no prominent signal is observed above 900 m/z, proving that the CDs are mainly composed of not very large molecular fragments, which indicates that small molecular fragments are major ingredients of CDs. The point was compared with previous results in Table S4. Additionally, no free PPDA (m/z = 108 Da) was detected in MS of these CDs, as shown in a pertinently magnified portion of MS (Fig. S5b). The MS displayed in Figs. S5b and c shows that there are molecular skeletons formed by polymerides of PPDA, such as dipolymer, proven by the m/z = 211 (M-H+), and tripolymer removing an amino group, proven by m/z = 304 (M-H+). It is suggested that the CDs may be formed by breaking of NH bonds and CH bonds of PPDA and re-formation of CN/CN bonds, being accompanied by the formation of OH, CO, CO owing to the existence of oxygen atoms in the solvent of ethanol and air under high pressure and temperature conditions.

FT-IR spectra of PPDA and CDs were acquired to get further insight into the surface functional groups of CDs, as shown in Fig. 3a. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of primary amine at 3373 and 3302 cm−1 show the essence of aromatic primary amine in PPDA. Notably, new absorption bands at 3291 and 1458 cm−1 corresponding to aromatic secondary amine and CN are observed in CDs [26]. New characteristic bands emerging at 1733, 1227 and 1248 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of CO, phenolic hydroxyl and aromatic ethers, respectively [27], and the absorption bands at 2854 and 2925 cm−1 are assigned to CH.

In addition, FT-IR of KH-792 and CD/silica composites are compared to verify silica formation, as shown in Fig. 3b. Before the formation of silica, FT-IR spectrum of KH-792 shows Si O C vibration at 1084 cm−1. However, in the CD/silica composites, vibrations of Si O Si at 1023 and 1124 cm−1 appear, together with a weak band of SiOH at 931 cm−1, which is due to the hydrolysis condensation of KH-792 to form a cross-linked silica network. The broad characteristic band located at 3421 cm−1 is attributed to a stretching vibration of hydrophilic OH and N H. Moreover, the bands at 1611 and 1466 cm−1 of CD/silica composites represent stretching vibrations of aromatic ring and CN for CDs, respectively. From above observation, it can be suggested that the phosphors are comprised of silica and CDs, where silica serves as matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CD/silica composites
ce:section.ce:para: First, UV–vis absorption and PL excitation (PLE) measurements were performed for determining optical properties of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CD ethanol solution has a broad absorption even extending to visible region, including three peaks centered at 220, 287 and 355 nm in the UV region and a peak centered at 507 nm in the visible region. The peaks at 220, 287 and 355 nm are ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, CN and n-π* transition of CO, respectively [28]. The peak at 507 nm is attributed to Mie scattering caused by CDs, which represents the typical absorptions of an aromatic system and indicates an extended conjugation in the CD structure [29]. Beyond that, the PLE spectrum shows two peaks at 356 and 504 nm, which are consistent with the peaks at 355 and 507 nm in the absorption spectrum in Fig. 4a.

As shown in Fig. 4b, with increasing excitation wavelength from 365 to 525 nm, the PL emission peak at 600 nm remains nearly unshifted, and the PL intensity reaches a maximum with excitation at the optimal excitation wavelength of 505 nm, which demonstrates the excitation-independent property of CDs. It is generally agreed that the surface states of CDs significantly affect PL properties, i.e., surface functional groups primarily respond to trap the excitons under excitation, and the radiative recombination of these surface-trapped excitons leads to the specific PL with corresponding energy [30]. Consequently, CDs with uniform surface states afford PL with unvaried energies under different excitation wavelengths. Therefore, the excitation-independent property implies uniform surface status of CDs [31]. In addition, the strong emission at 600 nm is obtained upon excitation at 365 nm with large Storks shift of 235 nm, which could be attributed to the phenyl-containing molecular structure [32].

PL properties of CDs largely depend on the band gap between their highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [33]. Exciton transition occurs after absorbing photons with energies greater than the band gap energy. The long-wavelength emission at 600 nm is supposed to originate mainly from a small energy gap of CDs, which might be caused by abundant amino-functional groups attached on the aromatic core of CDs [34]. The first principle calculations suggest that amino edge termination transforms into additional interband within the energy gap because of p orbital hybridization of CN at the edge sites [35]. In addition, amino-terminated groups have a higher HOMO than hydrogen-terminated groups in CDs because of their strong orbit interaction between the delocalized π orbit and the molecule orbit in the amino groups, resulting in a narrow optical band gap, which leads to a long emission wavelength of CDs [36]. The optical band gap of CDs is calculated according to UV–vis spectrum for verifying this result by Eq. (2) [37].
(2)
E g
= 1242 /
λ
o n s e t
where λ oneset is obtained by drawing the tangent of absorption and extrapolating to the horizontal axis. As given in Fig. S6a, λ oneset is determined as 579 nm and E
g is calculated as 2.15 eV. The E
g was further measured using the following Eq. (3) derived by Tauc and Mott independently:
(3)
Ahν = K
( hν −
E g
)
n
where A is the absorption coefficient, hν is the energy of the incident light, E
g is the optical energy gap and n is a number which characterizes the optical absorption processes, that is, n is equal to 1/2 for direct transition and 2 for indirect transition [38]. As A obeys Eq. (3) in the high absorbing region, it would be reasonable to determine the band gap of CDs by plotting (Ahν)2 versus photon energy (hν) and extrapolating the linear regions of this curve to (Ahν)2 = 0. So the value of E
g was obtained as 2.20 eV, which verifies the data of E
g (Fig. S6b). On the basis of these results, E
g is determined as 2.20 eV for CDs emitting orange-red light with its peak at 600 nm (corresponding to 2.07 eV of energy). The above-mentioned characterizations demonstrate the small optical energy gap of as-prepared CDs, which is responsible for long emission of CDs.

CDs should have efficient resistance against solid-state quenching to satisfy their practical application in LEDs. To analyze optical properties of solid-state CDs, CD powder was obtained by drying a CD ethanol solution. The CD ethanol solution exhibits intense orange-red light under UV light, as seen in Fig. 5 a’. However, no obvious fluorescence from CD powder is observed when illuminated by UV light as a result of fluorescence quenching in the solid state (Fig. 5b’). To restrain self-quenching of solid-state CDs, KH-792 was injected to CD aqueous solution to form CD/silica composites. As given in Fig. 5c’, d’, orange-red emissions are visualized from CD/silica composites under UV light irradiation.

PL spectra of CD powder and CD/silica composites were measured to further investigate the self-quenching PL of CD powder and solid-state PL of CD/silica composites. As seen in Fig. 5e, the CD ethanol solution exhibits strong PL emission at 600 nm, while no PL emission was detected from CD powder, i.e. CD powder suffers from PL quenching. In Fig. 5f, compared with the PL emission peak (at 600 nm) of the CD ethanol solution, a slight blue shift (12 nm) of PL maximum centered at 588 nm was observed for CD/silica film. PL properties of CDs are related to the surface states, so the observed difference in PL spectra may be ascribed to influence of the hydrolysis product of KH-792. Surface passivation of CDs by amino may change the distribution of surface emissive trap site on the CD surface, resulting in an increased ratio of PL emission between intrinsic core states (high emission energy) and surface states (low emission energy) [39]. The excitation-independent PL emission at 597 nm of CD/silica powder can be seen in Fig. 5g, which is similar with the emission of the CD ethanol solution. Therefore, there are no remarkable differences in emission between the CD solution and CD/silica composites. In order to further verify the feasibility of KH-792 as the solid dispersing agent for LEDs, the UV–vis spectrum of the hydrolysis product of KH-792 was recorded, as shown in Fig. 5h. The hydrolysis product of KH-792 has no apparent absorption above 300 nm, indicating that matrix absorbs neither the light emitted from LED chip nor the emission of CDs, which leads to efficient PL of CD/silica composites. In short, PL quenching of CD powder induced by aggregation can be restrained by embedding in silica matrix. Therefore, silica is a promising candidate as solid-state matrix to restrain fluorescence quenching from solid CDs.

High red-green-blue (RGB) spectral composition of CDs can promote the conversion of UV light to white light, which is desired in preparation of white LEDs. In order to calculate and compare the RGB spectral composition of samples, the PL spectra of CD ethanol solution, CD/silica film and CD/silica powder were divided into six colors (red for 650–700 nm, orange for 590–650 nm, yellow for 570–590 nm, green for 495–570 nm, blue for 450–495 nm and violet for 380–450 nm). High RGB spectral composition of 96.33%, 80.48% and 86.82% for CD ethanol solution, CD/silica film and CD/silica powder, respectively, is shown in Fig. S7, which is greatly higher than the RGB spectral composition reported previously [40]. Compared with the RGB spectral composition of CD ethanol solution, that of CD/silica film decreases by 15.85% and that of CD/silica powder decreases by 9.51%, which may be caused by the slight blue shift of PL spectra. Nevertheless, the CD/silica composites still possess high RGB spectral composition to realize the potential application in white LEDs with low CCT.

The QY of CD powder, CD/silica film and CD/silica powder was measured by the absolute method. The absolute QY value of a CD ethanol solution was also measured to study the difference between QY values of four samples. The absolute QY value of the CD ethanol solution is up to 52.46%, which is higher than that of CDs emitting a long wavelength in previous reports [41]. However, the QY value of CD powder is just 0.73%, which further convinces the fluorescence quenching of solid-state CDs. By embedding CDs in a silica matrix, the QY values of CD/silica film and CD/silica powder were measured as 38.18% and 41.72%, respectively. In addition, it is noteworthy that this absolute QY value of 52.46% of the CD ethanol solution is higher than the relative QY value of 36.13%, which is ascribed to the difference from measuring methods. This phenomenon was also reported in previous research [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the PL quenching and resuming
ce:section.ce:para: To explain the PL quenching of CDs, CD solutions with different concentrations were used to investigate the mechanism of PL concentration quenching. From the concentration-dependent PL shown in Fig. 6 a and b, the PL intensity increases up to the maximum value at 0.04 mg/mL as the concentration of CDs increases. When the concentration of CDs is high enough, PL quenching occurs and the PL intensity decreases sharply. The PL quenching may originate from Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) ascribed from particle aggregation as well as an inner filter effect at a high concentration. On one hand, aggregation of CD particles could lead to formation of clusters with a poor dispersion of CDs, which would induce FRET and hence result in a reduction of fluorescence intensity [43]. On the other hand, the self-absorption of emission photons and subsequent re-emission (inner filter effects) may induce PL quenching in a concentrated solution of CDs whose absorption and emission spectra are in an overlap [44]. To confirm the aggregation phenomenon of CDs in a high concentration, TEM was used to characterize the particle distribution of diverse concentrations of CD ethanol solutions. TEM images in Fig. 6c–e illustrate the effect of CD concentration on aggregation of CDs. It can be seen that when CD concentration was 0.001 mg/mL, CDs are nearly monodisperse with little aggregation. However, the aggregation of CDs appears with an increase of CD concentration up to 0.01 mg/mL and more aggregation is found at 0.1 mg/mL of CD solution. On the basis of these results, it can be suggested that CD particles are in direct contact with each other in CD powder, i.e., CD particles are close enough to each other, so that they are within Forster distance, therefore, the first one of requirements of FRET is met. Furthermore, the large overlap between the absorption spectrum of acceptor (CDs) and emission spectrum of donor (CDs), the second one of the primary conditions for FRET, is shown in Fig. 6f, in which the absorption of CDs overlaps partly with the emission of CDs, especially in the 540–600 nm wavelength span. Consequently, a combination of aggregation and spectra overlapping leads to an FRET and PL quenching in CD powder.

The function of silica matrix on PL recovery of CDs was studied. From Fig. 2b, as characterized by TEM, the morphology of CD/silica composites illuminates that the CDs are embedded into the silica matrix evenly without aggregation, which can also be further confirmed by an AFM image of CD/silica composites (Fig. S8). The average distance between adjacent CD particles in the silica matrix was evaluated to be about 15 nm according to the TEM image of CD/silica composites in Fig. 6g. This value of 15 nm exceeds the range of 1–10 nm, an acceptable separation for FRET [45]. As a result, FRET is suppressed and PL self-quenching restrained. Therefore, it is suggested that CDs in well dispersed state exhibit well-preserved PL.

To acquire further insight into the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of solid CDs and the effects of silica as a solid matrix on restraining self-quenching fluorescence, the multidimensional time-correlated single photon counting (TPSPC) method was adopted to evaluate the PL decay process of samples upon the excitation of 375 nm. The PL decay curves were fitted and an amplitude weighted average lifetime (τ
avg ) in the entire PL decay process was calculated according to Eq. (4) and Eq. (5) [46].
(4)
I
( t )
=
B 1
e
−
τ 1
/ τ
+
B 2
e
−
τ 2
/ τ
(5)
τ
a v g
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
where τ
1
, τ
2 represent the decay time constants, and B
1 , B 2 the pre-exponential factors for the time resolved decay lifetime of τ
1 and τ
2 , respectively.

Then, the radiative decay rates (k
r ) and non-radiative decay rates (k
nr ) of all the samples were calculated by using known QY and τ
avg with Eqs. (6) and (7):
(6)
k r
k r
+
k
n r
= QY
(7)
k r
+
k
n r
=
1
τ
a v g

The aggregation of CDs from a high concentration of CD solution and CD powder can also be proved by PL lifetime. The change of PL lifetime is due to the difference in k
nr . All of the parameters are summarized in Table 1 . As given in Fig. 7 a and Table 1, the CD ethanol solution with concentration of 0.001 mg/mL shows double-exponential decay with
τ
a v g
of 9.58 ns and k
nr of 4.92 × 107 s−1, while
τ
a v g
decreases to 8.87 ns when the concentration of CDs increases to 1 mg/mL. The CD powder shows double-exponential decay with
τ
a v g
of 1.64 ns and k
nr of 6.05 × 108 s−1. The decrease in
τ
a v g
with an increase of concentration verifies the occurrence of FRET in CD aggregates. This is because FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer process, which will decrease PL lifetime. In contrast, an inner filter effect is a radiative process induced by emission and reabsorption of photons, so the lifetime is mostly independent of inner filter effects [44]. Therefore, in a comprehensive point of view, the change of PL lifetime further suggests the presence of aggregation in high concentration of CD solution and CD powder.

By physically embedding CDs in a silica matrix, the
τ
a v g
increases to 9.16 ns and k
nr greatly decreases to 5.93 × 107 s−1, meaning that the silica matrix can effectively disperse CDs, which suppresses non-radiative process and avoids the PL quenching induced by the aggregation of CDs. Meanwhile, CD/silica powder shows
k
n r
of 5.98 × 107 s−1 and
τ
a v g
of 9.75 ns, in accordance with CD/silica film.

On the basis of above results, a mechanism of PL self-quenching in solid-state CDs and the resistance against solid-state quenching of CDs through physical embedding into a silica matrix is illustrated in Fig. 8 . In the CD ethanol solution, CD particles uniformly disperse in ethanol, which leads to a long distance among CD particles. As a result, a dilute CD ethanol solution emits strong PL. However, CD particles directly contact with each other in aggregate-state powder, so the distance among CD particles is shortened to less than Foster distance, and the FRET accompanying aggregation causes PL self-quenching. In CD/silica composites, the distance among CD particles is extended because of effective dispersion of silica. Thus the CD particles embedded in the silica keep an appropriate distance (about 15 nm) from each other (outranging Foster distance, 1–10 nm) [45], which avoids the FRET phenomenon and consequently the fluorescence quenching of solid-state CDs. This may outline a fundamental principle for achieving resistance against solid-state self-quenching of CDs being supported by solid matrix.

The phosphors should have excellent thermostability for application in LEDs. Therefore, a TG analysis was conducted under a nitrogen atmosphere to evaluate thermostability of samples. The curves in Fig. 9 a reveal a weight loss of 3.07% from CD powder at 150 °C, which may be ascribed to the dehydration and release of the surface functional groups on CDs. On the contrary, CD/silica composites have negligible decomposition up to 200 °C, suggesting that they are thermostable enough to be applied in LEDs with an operation temperature below 150 °C [47]. Fig. 9b shows the PL spectra of CD/silica film as a function of temperature (30–150 °C). In Fig. 9c, the changes in emission intensity and wavelength are plotted. It can be seen that the PL intensity increases with a temperature increase from 30 to 110 °C, but then decreases at higher temperatures, which is mainly due to high-temperature induced PL quenching [48]. In addition, it reveals a monotonic blueshift of wavelength when temperature increases from 30 to 90 °C, and no shift occurs when temperature has a further increase from 90 to 150 °C, indicating a renormalization of band energies due to an electron-phonon interaction at high temperature [49]. The maximum shift is 1.3% upon heating from room temperature to 90 °C and when temperature is returned back to room temperature, PL wavelength is restored.

In addition, in practical application of LEDs, phosphors are often exposed to UV light for a long time, thus outstanding light stability of phosphors is also required. The UV-stability of CD/silica composites was evaluated by continuous irradiation under UV light for an hour. As given in Fig. 9d, the PL intensity of CD/silica composites keeps nearly constant even after 60 min of irradiation, which demonstrates good UV-stability of the composites.

To explore the potential of as-prepared CD/silica composites which have exhibited excellent optical properties and film-forming property, a white LED with the obtained CD/silica composites as a white light converter and UV chip as excitation light was fabricated. Fig. 10 a shows a schematic view of the LED based on CD/silica composites. It can be seen that LEDs are mainly composed of a UV chip, gold wire, lens and electrodes. Photoluminescent CD/silica composites are coated on the lens as a color converter. The UV chip is made up of n-type semiconductor dominated by electrons and p-type semiconductor dominated by holes. One end of the chip is connected to cathode and the other end to anode of the power source. When electric current is applied to the UV chip, a p-n junction will form, which promotes electron-hole recombination and energy release by photon, and subsequently CD/silica composites absorb the excitation light from the UV chip and re-emit broad visible light peaked at the orange-red region, which will eventually convert the UV light into warm white light. The spectra of the LED were recorded under various working voltages, as given in Fig. 10b. As the voltage increases from 2.9 to 3.3 V, the emission intensity of the LED gradually increases and reaches its maximum value at 3.3 V. When the working voltage of the LED exceeds 3.3 V, the corresponding intensity of white LED is beyond the detection range of the PR 655 photometer.

By combining the CD/silica film and a UV chip, an LED rendering bright warm white light is presented. As shown in Fig. 10c, the fabricated white LED produces a wide emission spectrum covering almost the entire visible region from 400 to 720 nm. The emission band at 400 nm is from UV chip and the wide emission ranging from 450 to 720 nm is from the emission of CD/silica composites. The inset in Fig. 10c is the photograph of the working white LED with warm white light emission. From Fig. 10d, it can be seen that the CIE coordinates (0.44, 0.42) and corresponding CCT (2951 K) of as-prepared LED are located at a warm white light zone in favor of indoor illumination [50]. The LED in present study has a low CCT because of its relatively high proportion of orange and red light. Thus, it is suggested that the CD/silica composites emitting orange-red light in solid state be a promising candidate as phosphors for lighting application.

A simple and universal approach has been established for restraining PL quenching from solid-state CDs through physical embedding CDs into a silica matrix. CD particles in CD/silica composites keep an appropriate distance from each other, which can suppress the FRET and PL quenching induced by aggregation of CDs. CDs were synthesized through one-step solvothermal method using PPDA as precursor and ethanol as solvent. The prepared CDs have exhibited excitation-independent emission with a peak wavelength at 600 nm under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 365 to 525 nm. The CD ethanol solution has shown a high QY value of 52.46% under the optimum excitation wavelength of 505 nm, and a high RGB spectral composition of 96.33% upon excitation at 365 nm. There exist oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups in the obtained CDs with sizes of 4.0–9.0 nm. Through CD powder has shown obvious PL quenching, it can regain PL after being embedded into the silica matrix to achieve a high QY value of 41.72%. In addition, CD/silica composites have exhibited outstanding film-forming ability through a simple heating process. These CD/silica composites have shown a slight mass loss up to 200 °C and nearly constant PL intensity when exposed to continuous UV irradiations for 1 h. Benefiting from the superior film-forming ability, thermo- and UV-stability, as-prepared CD/silica film has been employed to construct a warm white LED by combining with a UV chip. The white LED has CIE coordinates of (0.44, 0.42) and CCT of 2951 K, which is favorable for an application in indoor illumination. Therefore, this work provides directive significance for efficient resistance against PL quenching of solid-state CDs and the potential application prospect of CD/silica composites in lighting systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1610255, U1607120), the Shanxi Provincial Key Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology (2015013002-10, 201605D131045-10), and the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program (201603D111010 and 201703D321015-1).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
mmc1 mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.10.041.
